task: A coworker calls out right before a shift starts, and the schedule is already tight. I’m trying to figure out how to respond and what to do next so the work still gets covered.

turns.agent_response:
Yes — keep it simple and cover the gap fast.

## First steps
1. **Acknowledge it**
   - make sure the call-out is seen/heard
   - don’t waste time arguing it

2. **Tell the right person**
   - manager / lead / supervisor
   - whoever can shift coverage

3. **Find the hole**
   - what station
   - what time
   - how long the gap is

4. **Cover the most important part first**
   - keep the busiest or most critical station covered
   - delay lower-priority tasks if needed

---

## What to do next
- ask who can **split the shift**
- ask who can **float** for a bit
- move nonessential work off the missing person’s station
- simplify the plan for the shift

---

## Simple rule
**Name the gap, cover the gap, drop the extras.**

---

## Tiny memory version
**Call it, fill it, simplify it.**

If you want, I can make this into a **manager/crew cheat sheet** for last-minute call-outs.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s the idea. But in real life it’s messier than that. If one person calls out and the kitchen’s already slammed, the question is who’s actually gonna move, not just “tell the right person.” 

I’d want something more like:
- who’s the closest backup
- what can get dropped without wrecking the whole shift
- what has to stay covered no matter what
- and who’s gonna get stuck doing the extra work

Because usually it ends up on the same few people anyway. If you want, make it more like a quick shift plan, not a general checklist.

turns.agent_response:
Yes — here’s the **real shift version**.

## Quick plan
### 1) Find the closest backup
- the person already nearby
- the one who knows the station
- the one who can move fastest without breaking their own work

### 2) Keep only the must-cover work
- the busiest station
- the safety-critical stuff
- anything that will blow up if it sits

### 3) Drop the extras
- side prep
- deep cleaning
- nice-to-have restock
- anything that can wait without hurting service

### 4) Say who’s absorbing the extra
- one person owns the gap
- don’t spread it to everyone
- don’t let the same two people get buried silently

---

## Simple shift rule
**Closest backup first. Must-cover stays. Extras wait. Name the load.**

---

## Tiny memory version
**Back up the gap. Protect the rush. Drop the rest.**

If you want, I can turn this into a **super short line-cook version** or a **manager version**.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, this is closer. But I’d want it a little more real-world, like what actually gets said in the kitchen.

Something like:
- who’s closest and already moving
- who can slide over without making a second mess
- what station can limp along for a bit
- what absolutely can’t be left alone
- and who’s getting the extra load, because somebody always does

The part about naming the load matters. Otherwise it just disappears onto the same people and nobody says it out loud.

If you can, make it into a short shift note somebody could actually read in 10 seconds.

turns.agent_response:
Yep — here’s the **10-second kitchen version**:

**Closest backup first.  
Keep the must-cover station up.  
Let the weakest station limp.  
Drop the extras.  
Name who’s taking the load.**

### Even shorter:
**Move the nearest body. Keep the hot spot covered. Drop the rest. Say who owns it.**

If you want, I can make that into a **manager voice** version or a **line cook voice** version.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that works. The first one especially — that sounds like something somebody would actually say in the kitchen. Short, plain, no extra fluff. 

Closest backup first. Keep the must-cover station up. Let the weakest station limp. Drop the extras. Name who’s taking the load.

That’s the one.
